dropped.location: tasks/nebula-frontend.md:427 (step 3 — #onDagChanged synthesizes getState() and svc.broadcasts); dag-tree.ts:194/228/etc (#onChanged() called inside/after each mutation); star.ts:42,484
dropped.title: #onDagChanged fires svc.broadcast synchronously inside the SQL-mutating dagTree() traversal — opening an input gate / interleaving hazard not addressed
dropped.finding: Each DagTree mutation calls this.#onChanged() (dag-tree.ts:194,228,289,...) which the constructor wires to () => this.#onDagChanged() (star.ts:42). Design B makes #onDagChanged call dagTree.getState() (which calls #requireAuth, dag-tree.ts:410) and svc.broadcast to ALL TreeSubscribers — all within the synchronous unwind of the single @mesh dagTree() call, after the storage.transactionSync block but before the awaited method returns to the client. The resource broadcast (#broadcast, star.ts:513) runs from a DIFFERENT place: after `await this.#resources.transaction(...)` in the async doTransaction handler (star.ts:276-277), i.e. at a well-defined post-commit point in an async handler. The orgTree broadcast instead fires mid-traversal from a sync mutation method. svc.broadcast fires one-way lmz.calls; whether issuing them from inside the still-executing mesh-method body (vs after it returns) is safe — re: input-gate opening, callContext liveness during the one-way dispatch, and ordering relative to the method's own response delivery — is not analysed anywhere in the spec. durable-objects.md warns that await/setTimeout inside a DO opens input gates; svc.broadcast's dispatch mechanics relative to the enclosing sync method should be confirmed.
dropped.why: The finding's structural description is accurate (verified: dag-tree.ts mutation methods are synchronous — `createNode(...): number` at :173, no `async`; each calls `this.#onChanged()` AFTER its `transactionSync` block at :194/228/247/289/etc; star.ts:42 wires that to `#onDagChanged()`, currently an empty no-op at :484-486; the resource `#broadcast` instead fires via `onMutations` AFTER the `transactionSync` closes inside the *async* `Resources.transaction`, resources.ts:545-547 / star.ts:277). But the load-bearing hazard claim is REFUTABLE on read. The finder fears `svc.broadcast` "opens an input gate that lets a concurrent dagTree() mutation interleave before the broadcast's getState() snapshot is serialized." I read `packages/mesh/src/broadcast.ts` (the `broadcast()` factory, :131-186): the dispatch is FULLY SYNCHRONOUS — it loops over targets and calls `doInstance.lmz.call(...)` fire-and-forget (3-arg, or 4-arg with `onErrorOnly`), with NO `await`, `setTimeout`, or `setInterval` anywhere. `getState()` (dag-tree.ts:409-412) just reads `this.#cached` synchronously. So the entire `#onDagChanged → getState() → svc.broadcast → lmz.call` sequence runs with zero yield points; per durable-objects.md, input gates open only on await/setTimeout/setInterval — none exist here, so no gate opens and nothing can interleave before the snapshot is dispatched. The finder hedges ("whether ... is safe ... is not analysed") and the suggestion is merely "confirm ... is safe" — a confirm-during-build ask, not a demonstrated defect. The residual "please document the dispatch-from-inside-a-sync-mesh-method safety" point is near-nil value: this is exactly the blessed pattern the resource `#broadcast` already uses (a synchronous callback firing `svc.broadcast` from inside an enclosing mesh method), and `svc.broadcast` is explicitly DESIGNED for synchronous fire-and-forget dispatch (broadcast.ts header + BroadcastFn returns void). Not in the prior digest as a hazard — SP-14/SP-DROP-2/SP-DROP-3 cover the same step-3 design but are test-coverage / trigger-naming / live-snapshot-not-cloned, none touching concurrency safety — so it is NEW, but the asserted mechanism is factually wrong and the leftover is already answered by an existing approved pattern.

dropped.location: dag-tree.ts:409-411 (getState returns this.#cached) + :83-103 (#cached/#invalidate); nebula-frontend.md:427-428 (synth from dagTree.getState())
dropped.title: getState() returns the live #cached object — orgTree broadcast serializes shared mutable state; confirm no mutation between synth and structured-clone
dropped.finding: getState() returns this.#cached directly (dag-tree.ts:411) — the live cached DagTreeState (nodes Map, edges Set, nested permissions Map), not a clone, and #invalidate() nulls it on the next mutation (:100-103). The orgTree broadcast synthesizes the snapshot from getState() and hands it to svc.broadcast, which structured-clones at dispatch. In the single-mutation-then-broadcast flow this is fine (the broadcast reads the freshly-rebuilt cache). But the design includes the originator and broadcasts to ALL clients on EVERY change; if any future code path (or a batched/multi-op tree mutation) were to mutate-invalidate-rebuild between the getState() call and svc.broadcast's serialization, subscribers could receive a different generation than the one the mutation produced. Prior digest SP-DROP-3 noted the same 'live cached object, not a clone' fact but it was dropped in verification; re-raising narrowly as: the design relies on svc.broadcast serializing synchronously before the next mutation can rebuild #cached, and that assumption is unstated.
dropped.why:
This re-raises SP-DROP-3 (prior digest line 152), which was already DROPPED in skill-panel verification, and the re-raise does not meet the "prior fix is wrong / half-applied" bar — the prior drop was correct and nothing has changed to invalidate it.

Facts verified by reading the cited files:
- dag-tree.ts:411 getState() does return this.#cached (the live cached object, not a clone), and #invalidate() (lines 100-103) nulls #_cached so #buildState() (105-141) rebuilds fresh Maps/Sets on next access. The finder's factual premise is accurate.
- nebula-frontend.md:427 (design-B step 3) does specify #onDagChanged synthesizing dagTree.getState() then svc.broadcast. star.ts:484-486 confirms #onDagChanged is currently an empty no-op (the design isn't built yet — this is in-scope v3 spec).

Why the concern does not survive: I read packages/mesh/src/broadcast.ts:131-185. The broadcast(...) function body is fully synchronous — the direct path (targets ≤ directThreshold) immediately loops doInstance.lmz.call(...) for every target with NO await, and the tree path is a single synchronous lmz.call to the tier worker. Structured-clone serialization happens inside that synchronous lmz.call envelope construction. Per JS run-to-completion plus the DO "keep methods synchronous" rule (durable-objects.md), control cannot return to another tree mutation between the getState() read and the synchronous svc.broadcast dispatch — there is no await, no input-gate-opening boundary. So the "mutate-invalidate-rebuild between getState() and serialization" race the finder posits is structurally impossible in the specced flow, not merely "fine for now." The finder themselves concede "the single-mutation-then-broadcast flow this is fine" and rate it minor/medium.

The "unstated invariant" the suggestion asks to document (svc.broadcast snapshots synchronously before the next mutation) is a structural guarantee of the mesh transport (synchronous one-way lmz.call dispatch, ADR-003) and JS run-to-completion — not a fragile assumption the design rests on. The hypothetical that could break it ("a future batched/multi-op tree mutation that mutates between getState() and broadcast") is a non-existent code path the current design (synth once, broadcast once per #onDagChanged) does not contain. The suggested probe ("two rapid mutations deliver two distinct generations") is also already substantially covered: nebula-frontend.md:440 test (c) and :542 assert mutation→broadcast fidelity reaching second client + originator, and each broadcast serializes a fresh #buildState() rebuild. This is speculative defensive hardening against a future refactor, re-raising an already-dropped finding without demonstrating the prior drop was wrong.

dropped.finding: ADR-002 requires every surface (incl. the wire) to round-trip the full structured-clone value space — here `nodes: Map`, `edges: Set`, `permissions: Map<number, Map<...>>`. The digest (SP-DROP-3) notes getState() returns the live cached DagTreeState (Maps/Sets), not a clone. The broadcast carries it across `svc.broadcast` → structured-clone encode → each client's handleOrgTreeUpdate. Two ADR-002 concerns the v3 spec leaves implicit: (1) the structured-clone wire path must encode/decode the nested `Map<number, Map<string, tier>>` faithfully (the 5.3.8 probe (b)/:542 asserts this — good), but (2) because getState() hands out the LIVE cached object, a broadcast in flight while DagTree mutates again could encode a half-mutated Map (the broadcast encode is not guaranteed atomic w.r.t. a subsequent synchronous mutation). The single-threaded DO makes this safe in practice if the broadcast encode completes synchronously before returning to the event loop, but the spec never states that the synth must snapshot/clone before broadcasting, and an async broadcast path would expose torn reads of the live Maps.

The finder does not invoke the wrong-resolution re-raise exception (priorOverlap is null; SP-DROP-3 is cited as support, not as a flawed fix), and the only suggested action is a doc note for a non-bug. dropped.location: factory-conflict-outcome.md:10 (human-in-the-loop advances baseline via api.accept) + :11 (held-fanout disposition: human-in-the-loop in the DROP set) + :26 (invariant 8: hold covers reconnect snapshot 'if the resource still has pending optimistic state')

dropped.finding: human-in-the-loop is specified to (a) advance the baseline via `api.accept(snapshot)` and release the queue key (build finding, :10), AND (b) DROP any held fanout (:11, it's in the 'incorporated server state or committed' DROP set). But the resource's optimistic paint STAYS (the user's edit is still on screen) and the app owes a follow-up resolution transaction (api-reference.md:668). So after human-in-the-loop the resource is in a hybrid state: queue key idle, baseline advanced, BUT local store still diverges from server (painted optimistic value ≠ accepted server snapshot). Invariant 8's reconnect-snapshot hold is gated on 'the resource still has pending optimistic state (debounced write outstanding OR transaction in flight)' — after human-in-the-loop there is NEITHER (key released, no debounced write), yet the store still holds an un-submitted divergent value. A reconnect (or a fresh fanout) would therefore NOT be held and would clobber the painted-but-unsubmitted human-in-the-loop value via the unconditional write-through (Three handlers table: handleResourceUpdate 'yes, unconditional'). The 'review later' banner value silently vanishes on the next fanout/reconnect. The held-fanout DROP for human-in-the-loop is inconsistent with keeping the paint.
dropped.why:
Substantially the same as the already-reported-and-dropped SP-DROP-1 (prior digest line 150), which raised exactly this concern — `human-in-the-loop` sits in the held-fanout DROP set (factory-conflict-outcome.md:11) yet "did NOT incorporate or commit server state," so dropping the hold "silently discards a real concurrent change the user is being asked to reconcile." The current finding adds a reconnect/later-fanout refinement (the hybrid state: key idle + baseline advanced per :10, paint still divergent, invariant-8 hold at :26 gated on "debounced write outstanding OR transaction in flight" so a later fanout isn't held), but that refinement falls to the same refutation.

The cited spec text is factually accurate (I read :10 h-i-t-l advances baseline via api.accept; :11 h-i-t-l in the DROP set; invariant 8 :26 gated on pending optimistic state). The defect claim, however, misreads the canonical stash pattern. The finding's headline harm — "the 'review later' banner value silently vanishes on the next fanout/reconnect" — is false: the documented h-i-t-l pattern (resources.md:405-430) stashes BOTH local and server (`store.app.conflicts[eTag] = { local: resolution.local, server: resolution.server }`) into an app-owned structure, NOT into `store.resources.{rt}[{rid}].value`. A resource fanout's unconditional write-through (Three handlers, nebula-frontend.md:103) only touches `store.resources.*.value`/`.meta`, never `store.app.conflicts`, so the parked conflict (incl. the user's un-submitted local edit) is untouchable by clobber. The banner reads `store.app.conflicts`, not the reactive paint.

The finding's own suggestion ("pin that human-in-the-loop's stash lives outside the reactive store (a separate conflict-stash structure)") is ALREADY exactly the documented design. And the residual clobber (the reactive paint being overwritten by a later fanout) is anticipated/intended, not a bug: api-reference.md:274 explicitly says of the h-i-t-l resolution submit "The local store may have moved on; the stashed eTag is the right baseline" — the resolution deliberately resubmits using the stashed `conflict.server.meta.eTag` + stashed value via the Explicit eTag override (api-reference.md:269-285, resources.md:447-461), not the live store. So "store moved on" is the documented expected condition. The added reconnect angle changes nothing: SP-DROP-1's verifier rationale (concurrent change surfaced via stashed `resolution.server`, edit via stashed `resolution.local`) holds identically for a later reconnect snapshot. Net: a re-raise of a dropped item whose premise (divergent value lives only in the clobberable reactive paint) is a misreading; no new defect.</reason>
</invoke>

dropped.location: factory-conflict-outcome.md:11 (validation-failed/permission-denied/timeout/infrastructure-error APPLY the held snapshot once the key is idle) + :21 (invariant 3 rollback restores pre-write snapshot)
dropped.title: Held-fanout APPLY-when-idle on rollback conclusions can re-submit a server value the user never typed via the synced-state middleware
dropped.finding: On a pure-rejection rollback (validation-failed / permission-denied / timeout / infrastructure-error) the resource first rolls its optimistic value back to the pre-write snapshot (invariant 3), then APPLIES the held fanout snapshot 'once the key is idle' (:11). The held snapshot must be applied via the remote write-through path with `source:'remote'` so the synced-state middleware skips submission (factory-collection-sync.md:28 / debounce-serial-queue.md skip). But the timing — rollback writes the pre-write value through the store (a write that itself must be source-tagged to not submit), THEN applies the held snapshot — is two store writes in sequence on a now-idle key. If either the rollback restore or the held-snapshot apply is NOT source-tagged remote (the spec describes rollback as 'restore from snapshot' without naming the source tag), it re-enters the set-trap middleware and queues a fresh transaction submitting either the rolled-back value or the server's value back to the server as if the user typed it. The spec never states that the rollback-restore write carries `source:'remote'`.
dropped.why: The finding's core premise — that the rollback-restore and held-fanout-apply writes are NOT source-tagged, so they re-enter the set-trap middleware and re-submit the rolled-back/server value — is refuted by the contract surface. api-reference.md:714 defines `type WriteContext = { source: 'local' | 'remote' | 'rollback' | 'computed' }`, and :719-724 spell out exactly the two cases the finding worries about: (1) `'rollback'` = "framework restoring a pre-write value after a failed transaction. Synced-state middleware skips." (2) `'remote'` = "server fanout. Synced-state middleware skips." So the rollback restore IS source-tagged (as `'rollback'`, a dedicated tag for precisely this purpose, NOT untagged) and the held-fanout apply — being a server snapshot delivered through the framework write-through (nebula-frontend.md:432 "existing framework write-through (`source:'remote'`)"; :455 "the `{ source: 'remote' }` bypass"; factory-collection-sync.md:28 "a remote fanout that writes a collection through `{ source: 'remote' }` must NOT re-submit") — also skips submission. The finding's literal phrasing ("the spec never states the rollback-restore carries `source:'remote'`") is true only because the spec uses the MORE specific `'rollback'` tag that also skips; the substantive re-submission risk does not exist. The suggested fix ("pin that BOTH are `source:'remote'`") is itself slightly wrong (rollback is `'rollback'`). The test gap it raises is also already pinned: factory-conflict-outcome.md:43 has the green "held news released after a rollback conclusion" test, line 44's Mn7 v3-port obligation is the per-conclusion-kind held-fanout DROP-vs-APPLY matrix probe, and factory-collection-sync.md's "No echo" test asserts a remote write never re-submits. Not in the prior digest as such, but it's a misreading of the source-tag mechanism that the contract docs explicitly define.

dropped.location: debounce-serial-queue.md:97-98 ('Reconnect ⇒ suspended in-flight replays (SAME newETag...), then dirty free keys flush'); factory-conflict-outcome.md:26 (invariant 8: reconnect snapshot held while pending optimistic state)
dropped.title: Reconnect replays the in-flight transaction AND flushes held writes, but the order vs. the held reconnect-snapshot creates a window where the replay commits against a baseline the snapshot already superseded
dropped.finding: On reconnect the sequence is: (1) re-submit the in-flight transaction with its same newETag, (2) flush held dirty writes, while (3) auto-resubscribe pushes a fresh initial snapshot that invariant 8 says is HELD because the resource has pending optimistic state. The ordering of (1) replay vs (3) snapshot-push-and-hold is unspecified relative to each other across the two detour docs. If the auto-resubscribe snapshot arrives and is processed BEFORE the in-flight replay completes, the held snapshot sits buffered; the replay returns committed (idempotent short-circuit) — and then what releases/merges the held snapshot? Invariant 8 says 'the flushed write commits-or-conflicts-and-merges' — but a replay that idempotently short-circuits to committed did NOT go through the conflict resolver, so the held snapshot's merge step (which only runs on conflict) never fires. The held pre-disconnect-keystrokes-vs-reconnect-snapshot merge is only guaranteed when the flush CONFLICTS; when the replay commits clean the held snapshot is just dropped (committed is in the DROP set, :11), discarding the reconnect snapshot — which is correct only if the replay's committed value is actually newer than the held snapshot, which isn't guaranteed if a third party committed during the disconnect.
dropped.why:
The finding's central data-loss scenario — "the replay commits clean (idempotent short-circuit) AND the held reconnect snapshot is dropped, discarding a third party's commit that landed during the disconnect" — is impossible under the pinned semantics, so its conclusion is factually wrong even though it quotes real spec text.

Key refutation, from the pinned idempotency replay matrix in nebula-frontend.md:526-530 (ADR-005, forward-only eTags, no dedupe ledger): a replay resolves `committed` ONLY IF the replayed resource still carries the client's `newETag` at retry. Line 529 pins exactly the third-party-during-disconnect case: "**put / delete, churn-of-the-replayed-resource-itself**: `conflict` (current eTag != the op's baseline -> resolver fires with the third party's `currentSnapshot`), NOT `committed`." So a third-party commit during the disconnect churns the eTag, which forces the replay to resolve `conflict`, NOT the idempotent-`committed` short-circuit. The finding's two preconditions ("replay commits clean" and "a third party committed during the disconnect") are therefore mutually exclusive.

On the conflict path, the held snapshot is correctly handled: factory-conflict-outcome.md invariant 8 (line 26) explicitly specifies the reconnect ordering the finding claims is "unspecified" — "auto-resubscribe delivers a fresh server snapshot; if the resource still has pending optimistic state ... that snapshot is held too, then the flushed write commits-or-CONFLICTS-AND-MERGES." The held-fanout disposition rule (line 11) drops the hold on `committed` and supersedes/merges it via the conflict's server snapshot on `conflict` — which is precisely the third party's `currentSnapshot`. The merge step the finding says "never fires" DOES fire, because the replay resolves `conflict`, not `committed`, in exactly the scenario it worries about.

On the clean-`committed` path (no third party), dropping the held auto-resubscribe snapshot is correct: with the client's `newETag` still current, the server snapshot equals the client's own committed value — never newer. The finding even concedes the drop is "correct only if a third party did not commit during the disconnect"; since a third-party commit forces `conflict`, the unsafe conjunction never arises.

The ordering concern (snapshot processed before replay settles) is also covered: invariant 8 + invariant 10 hold the auto-resubscribe snapshot for ANY resource with pending optimistic state (writes held through the disconnect / in-flight replay), so it cannot be applied-then-dropped ahead of the replay's resolution. Adjacent prior items exist (R3-13 banner honesty; SP-18 held-fanout per-kind disposition test gap) but neither overlaps this specific claim; regardless, the claim is refuted on the merits, not merely already-reported.

dropped.location: debounce-serial-queue.md:74-75 (Idle on committed: 'baseline+base advance together') + :117 (txResultSuccess InFlightWithBuffer→InFlight re-anchors base); factory-conflict-outcome.md:23 (invariant 5 sites a + b)
dropped.title: First-divergence base capture fires on the first keystroke of a burst, but the burst boundary after a 'committed' transition (Idle→Pending) re-anchors base at commit — a keystroke arriving in the same tick as the commit settle has an ambiguous base
dropped.finding: Two base-capture sites can race at a commit boundary: site (a) first-divergence captures base = pre-first-keystroke value when a write hits an Idle key; site (b) commit-boundary re-anchors base to the committed value when a buffered write chains onto a clean commit. The state diagram shows two distinct paths out of InFlight on a committed result: (1) 'committed (no buffer)' → Idle [baseline+base advance together], and (2) 'committed ∧ dirty' → re-enter Pending [re-anchor base to committed value]. The hazard: a keystroke that arrives in the same microtask as the commit settle but is processed via the Idle path (because the buffer flag wasn't set yet) would capture base via site (a) = the OLD pre-burst value rather than the committed value, double-counting the user's own committed text on a later conflict (exactly the failure site (b) exists to prevent). Whether a write is classified as 'buffered during InFlight' (→ site b) vs 'fresh write to a just-Idled key' (→ site a) depends on the precise ordering of (commit-settle store write) vs (keystroke set-trap), which the microtask-defer middleware makes nondeterministic.
dropped.why:
Refuted against the validated spike (apps/nebula/spike/vue-factory/src/debounce-queue.ts), which is the reference design the v3 port translates, plus the cited docs. The claimed intra-tick race does not exist and the proposed fix is already implemented:

1) No JS-level race. The commit settle (onSettle, lines 356-371) runs as one synchronous, run-to-completion body that sets `ks.baselineETag = committedETag; ks.baseValue = submission.value;` (lines 362-363) BEFORE calling `config.onCommitted?.()` and BEFORE `releaseKey`. A keystroke `write()` cannot interleave into the middle of a synchronous forEach body — either the whole settle runs first or the whole write runs first. The finding conflates async dispatch *between* operations (the microtask-defer middleware) with interleaving *within* a single synchronous operation; the latter cannot happen.

2) Site (a) is structurally unreachable post-commit. `ensureBaseline` (lines 185-193) early-returns `if (ks.baselineETag !== undefined)`. After the first write to a key, `baselineETag` is set and never reset to undefined except by `noteRemoteSnapshot` (line 509), which deletes the key ONLY when `!ks.dirty && isFree(ks)` (an idle key adopting a *remote* fanout snapshot — not a commit boundary, and it re-inits from the *current* store, never the 'OLD pre-burst value'). So a same-tick post-commit keystroke hits the early-return and chains off the just-committed baseline; it cannot re-capture the stale pre-burst base via site (a). The finding's failure premise ('a keystroke processed via the Idle path would capture base via site (a) = the OLD pre-burst value') is impossible.

3) The suggested fix is already the implementation. 'Update base/baseline synchronously with the committed write-through' = exactly lines 362-363, set synchronously before any callback. There is ONE commit handler (the onSettle forEach); the diagram's two committed branches (debounce-serial-queue.md:74 no-buffer→Idle and :85 committed∧dirty→Pending) differ only in what releaseKey does afterward (re-arm timers vs go idle), NOT in base capture — base advances with baseline uniformly. The diagram annotation the finder cites ('[baseline+base advance together]', :75) literally states the guarantee the finder claims is missing.

Not a duplicate of prior B4 / R3-2 (those concern capture-*point* semantics — submit-time vs first-divergence — not a commit-boundary intra-tick race), in scope (v3 detour files), no ADR conflict (consistent with ADR-004/005 client-held base). The B4 commit-boundary re-anchor was even capable-of-failing verified (debounce-serial-queue.md:11,47). Distinct but factually wrong/misreading.

dropped.location: nebula-frontend.md:404 ('Race window: between auto-derive at call time and actual server submission, a fanout could update the local store... Same outcome as the previous manual-lookup pattern'); debounce-serial-queue.md:27 (flush submits using the in-flight tx's resulting eTag, not the pre-submit eTag)
dropped.title: Auto-derived eTag is read 'at call time' but the debounce queue submits later — the documented race claim ('same outcome as manual lookup') understates a new same-actor staleness the serial queue introduces
dropped.finding: The auto-derive item says eTag is derived 'at call time' and treats the call-time-to-submission race as identical to the old manual pattern. But the serial queue explicitly overrides the pre-submit eTag: a buffered write submits with the IN-FLIGHT transaction's resulting eTag (debounce-serial-queue.md:27), NOT the eTag captured at call time. So for the debounced v-model path the call-time auto-derived eTag is frequently DISCARDED and replaced by the queue's chained eTag — meaning the 'derive at call time' description in the auto-derive item is wrong for the dominant (debounced) path, and the 'same outcome as manual lookup' claim doesn't hold (the queue's eTag-chaining is a different, better mechanism). The contradiction is between two pinned items: auto-derive says 'call time', the queue says 'in-flight resulting eTag, not pre-submit'. An implementer reading only the auto-derive item would capture and submit the call-time eTag, breaking the serial-queue eTag chain and causing spurious self-conflicts on rapid typing.
dropped.why:
The finding's core premise — that the auto-derive item (nebula-frontend.md:392-404) and the serial-queue eTag-chaining (debounce-serial-queue.md:27) are "two pinned items" describing the SAME submission path and therefore contradict — is false. They describe disjoint paths.

(1) The auto-derive item governs the EXPLICIT `client.resources.transaction()` API (the OperationDescriptor shape at nebula-frontend.md:395-399 + api-reference.md:225-285; explicit-override cases = admin/scripting and human-in-the-loop). For a caller who omits `eTag`, the factory wrapper does a `store.resources.<typeName>[<rid>]?.meta?.eTag` lookup at call time (~20 LOC, :402). For THAT path the "Race window ... Same outcome as the previous manual-lookup pattern" claim (:404) is correct — auto-derive replicates the exact boilerplate lookup the caller used to write by hand; the fanout-between-lookup-and-submit race is genuinely identical.

(2) The serial-queue eTag-chaining governs the DEBOUNCED v-model path: synced-state middleware -> `queue.write` -> the queue tracks its OWN `baselineETag`, captured at first-divergence and advanced on commit/accept/resubmit, then submits via `client.transaction({ eTag: queueBaseline })`. Verified in the spike (the design is "implemented + tested"): apps/nebula/spike/vue-factory/src/create-nebula-client.ts:472-490 — the synced-state middleware calls `queue.write(...)`, NOT `client.resources.transaction()` with an OperationDescriptor; :426-432 — the queue's `submit` passes the queue baseline as an EXPLICIT `eTag: s.eTag`; debounce-queue.ts:188-189/:299/:362 — `baselineETag` is captured at first-divergence, submitted explicitly, advanced on commit. So the debounced path never invokes the auto-derive OperationDescriptor mechanism; there is no "call-time auto-derived eTag" on that path to discard.

Because the paths are disjoint, there is no contradiction between two pinned items, and the "implementer reading only the auto-derive item would capture/submit the call-time eTag and break the chain" scenario can't occur — whoever wires the debounced path follows debounce-serial-queue.md (its own detour file/state machine). The exact test the finding "suggests adding" (two rapid debounced writes -> second submits with the first's resulting eTag) already exists as a green D0 invariant (debounce-serial-queue.md:46: "the eTag of each submission equals the newETag ... of the previous successful submission"). The multi-resource "chain off batch eTag" angle was already reported as prior finding m9 (.round4-prior-digest.md:306-308). Net: a misreading that conflates two deliberately-separate submission paths.

dropped.location: tasks/nebula-frontend.md:417 ("Mesh sub-task (separate `packages/mesh` change — track with its own success criterion ...)")
dropped.title: Mesh sub-task for `ready` terminal-auth classification has no tracked owner/task file — a v3-blocking cross-package change is buried in a sub-bullet
dropped.finding: The `ready`-rejects-on-terminal-auth feature is explicitly stated to be UNACHIEVABLE in the factory alone: nebula-frontend.md:417/:418 say "the factory CANNOT fix this alone, since on first-connect terminal failure mesh emits no signal and goes to 'reconnecting', not 'disconnected'" — it requires a `packages/mesh` change (classify 401/403 in `#refreshToken` + `#connectInternal` catch). The item says to "track with its own success criterion," but no mesh task file is created/named, no checklist item exists in any tracked mesh work, and MEMORY/the task index has no entry for it. By contrast, the OTHER hard prerequisite (nebula-star-root-admin Part 1) IS a named task file (nebula-frontend.md:320). A v3 implementer reading the v3 checklist will try to wire `ready` rejection in the factory, find mesh emits no signal, and be blocked with no tracked mesh work to point to. Three 5.3.8 probes (nebula-frontend.md:535-536) depend on this mesh change landing.
dropped.why: FACTUALLY TRUE but ALREADY REPORTED (not a valid "prior fix wrong" re-raise). I verified nebula-frontend.md:417 — it does label "Mesh sub-task (separate `packages/mesh` change — track with its own success criterion; the factory CANNOT fix this alone...)" and there is genuinely no mesh task file or backlog entry for it (the three mesh task files — mesh-origin-request.md, mesh-active-callcontext-guard.md, mesh-call-tracing-and-ids.md — cover unrelated work; backlog.md §Lumenize Mesh has only a *successful-refresh test* item, not the first-connect 401 classification). So the underlying observation is real. BUT this exact concern was already raised TWICE in prior waves and is in the digest: (1) round-3 finding nebula-frontend-round3-findings-2026-06-11.json:917 part (2), digested as R3-DROP-1 — "The required change is inside packages/mesh/src/lumenize-client.ts ... a mesh-package work item, but it appears only as prose inside a nebula-frontend checklist bullet — the m14 'track by checklists' estimate method will miss it, exactly as it missed the nebula-auth global-refresh endpoint" — this was a deliberate DROP ("also already-considered"), not a fix that later drifted; (2) skill-panel SP-13 (digest L132) whose suggestion was literally "name the mesh lumenize-client.ts change as an explicit v3 sub-task with its own success criterion." The current :417 text IS the spec's response to those (it elevated the prose into a labeled sub-task with a named success criterion and promoted the three probes into 5.3.8, per the round-3 verifier note at JSON:997). The round-4 finding restates the same cross-package-work-buried-in-a-bullet concern and merely escalates the remedy from "named in-bullet sub-task" to "must be a separate file like nebula-star-root-admin Part 1." It does not invoke the prior-fix-wrong exception (priorOverlap claimed null) and presents no new evidence that the prior wave's deliberate choice — in-bullet tracking with a success criterion was acceptable — was mistaken. Substantially the same issue as R3-DROP-1/SP-13 → drop as already-reported.

synthesis.summary:
73 round-4 findings deduplicate to 60 distinct issues. The single most-flagged issue (6 lenses) is api-reference.md:78 still naming the nonexistent `AuthRequiredError` for `ready` rejection — a half-applied prior fix where the spec was corrected to mesh's `LoginRequiredError` but the contract doc was not. The `onShouldRefreshUI: null` opt-out footgun (3 lenses) is its sibling: the example does the opposite of its comment.

Six issues are flagged as blockers for v3 "go", clustering around two unresolved design holes plus three contract-doc data-incorrectness bugs:
1. The atomic-batch / per-resource-conflict-resolution mapping is genuinely undefined (ranks 1-2): the server transaction is all-or-nothing, but the client conflict engine resolves per resource and the spec validates a mixed-fate {use-server, permission-denied, committed} batch that all-or-nothing makes impossible. This is the load-bearing question for the flagship atomic addTodo and must be pinned before the engine is wired. maxRetries granularity (rank 18) is the same hole's third face.
2. Three contract docs (ranks 3, 4, 5) instruct a v3 implementer to build wrong/nonexistent behavior: the retries-exhausted rollback target contradicts the validated spike (restores an unrecoverable stale value), `AuthRequiredError` doesn't exist, and the headline default-flash feature has no decided DOM-element-discovery mechanism (its only binding API is deferred-post-5.3.7 and always-empty).
3. Two cross-package/cross-channel contracts are unspecified (ranks 6, 7, 8): the orgTree store envelope (`store.lmz.orgTree.value` reads vs bare-path writes — breaks the flagship tree example), the per-field debounce config wire format ("TBD during implementation" with a test already depending on it), and client.orgTree.* disconnect behavior (not connection-gated; non-idempotent createNode risks duplicate-on-replay).

The remaining majors/minors are mostly contract-doc self-contradictions (authScope derivation source, ready-terminal-state vs first-paint 'connecting' computed) and test-strategy gaps where probes aren't capable-of-failing (replay matrix in the MockClient list, rich-type e2e mis-labeled carry-forward, context.bindings empty-Map placeholder, held-fanout matrix asserting a provisional rule). Nits are stale spike line citations (file grew to 576 lines), the dag→orgTree rename residual, phantom spike/debounce/ directory, and anchor/typing drift.
themes: Half-applied prior fixes never propagated to api-reference.md (the declared contract): AuthRequiredError→LoginRequiredError, onShouldRefreshUI null-sentinel, retries-exhausted rollback target, authScope derivation source — the task-file narrative was corrected, the contract doc was not

Atomic server batch vs per-resource client conflict resolution mapping undefined — surfaces as re-submit batch composition (blocker), mixed-fate atomicity contradiction (blocker), and maxRetries granularity

orgTree dedicated channel under-specified vs the resource path it parallels: store envelope (.value vs bare), connection-gating asymmetry, non-idempotent-mutation replay, await-then-read ordering, subscriber-identity callChain end, @mesh-dispatch of both continuations
Default conflict-flash feature depends on a DOM-binding-discovery mechanism that is deferred-post-5.3.7 and always-empty — headline behavior with no decided way to find the elements to flash

Cross-package wire-format contracts left as inline 'TBD during implementation' with tests already depending on them (per-field debounce config; the nebula-studio compile-pass producer isn't a co-owned v3 item)

Connection-gate edge interactions under-specified: timeout-timer suspend/resume semantics, ontology-stale-drop vs once-guarded-reload, commit-boundary base re-anchor vs tombstones, explicit-transaction fold value precedence

Probes not capable-of-failing or mis-homed: replay matrix in the MockClient list (server-only logic), rich-type e2e mis-labeled carry-forward (zero existing coverage + old mock outcome shape), context.bindings empty-Map placeholder, held-fanout matrix asserting a rule the spec says will be re-reviewed

ready/auth-failure plumbing spans two packages — the 5.3.8 factory probe hard-depends on an unscheduled packages/mesh first-connect-401 classification change with no blocked-on marker, and the terminal 'disconnected' state collides with the first-paint 'connecting' computed

Stale spike references after the spike grew to 576 lines — every v3-port line citation off-target, the outcome-port item mis-describes the three coexisting outcome vocabularies, and LOC estimates under-scope the port

use-this re-conflict base re-anchoring is validated only for textMerge — counter/aggregate handlers (openCount +1) double-count across the chain, and the base/local/server snapshot identity/ownership contract (ADR-002) is unstated
Reconnect/disconnect test coverage rides WS-disconnect tooling that §5.3.6 says doesn't exist and no checklist item delivers — MockClient fakes the transition, over-claiming coverage
